SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The experiments are broadly comprehensive: 21 language-pair experiments across four families, zero-/one-shot settings, and relevant baselines (no-transfer and an unrelated-language regularizer) substantiate the main claims about related-language transfer. The paper includes useful sensitivity checks (ciphering to test surface correspondences and analysis of relatedness effects) and reports hyperparameters, and it explicitly discusses limitations about unrelated-language ‘‘transfer’’. However, reproducibility is weakened by no mention of public code or checkpoints and I see no report of multiple runs, variance estimates, or statistical testing to quantify significance—important for one-shot settings—so I deduct one point. | B(4): The experiments are broad (21 language pairs across four families, zero-/one-shot settings) and include thoughtful checks such as an unrelated-language baseline and the cipher perturbation to probe surface-form sensitivity, supporting the core claims about relatedness-driven transfer. The paper reports hyperparameters and discusses limitations (regularization effect of unrelated data), but it lacks explicit mention of released code and could use more systematic ablations/sensitivity analyses (e.g., varying amounts of high-resource data, architecturally focused ablations) to fully characterize when transfer succeeds or fails. Related work coverage is described as thorough, and failure modes are discussed, so overall the empirical grounding is solid with only minor gaps for full reproducibility and deeper analysis.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments are broadly comprehensive: 21 language-pair experiments across four families, zero-/one-shot settings, and relevant baselines (no-transfer and an unrelated-language regularizer) substantiate the main claims about related-language transfer. The paper includes useful sensitivity checks (ciphering to test surface correspondences and analysis of relatedness effects) and reports hyperparameters, and it explicitly discusses limitations about unrelated-language ‘‘transfer’’. However, reproducibility is weakened by no mention of public code or checkpoints and I see no report of multiple runs, variance estimates, or statistical testing to quantify significance—important for one-shot settings—so I deduct one point.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments are broad (21 language pairs across four families, zero-/one-shot settings) and include thoughtful checks such as an unrelated-language baseline and the cipher perturbation to probe surface-form sensitivity, supporting the core claims about relatedness-driven transfer. The paper reports hyperparameters and discusses limitations (regularization effect of unrelated data), but it lacks explicit mention of released code and could use more systematic ablations/sensitivity analyses (e.g., varying amounts of high-resource data, architecturally focused ablations) to fully characterize when transfer succeeds or fails. Related work coverage is described as thorough, and failure modes are discussed, so overall the empirical grounding is solid with only minor gaps for full reproducibility and deeper analysis.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper includes basic and informative baselines (monolingual no-transfer and an unrelated-language condition) and evaluates on multiple language pairs, which permits assessing the role of relatedness; it also analyzes confounds (e.g., ciphering) to separate true transfer from regularization. However, from the summary it appears to omit stronger or more recent cross-lingual and multilingual transfer baselines and alternative transfer techniques that would be natural competitors, and code/checkpoints are not mentioned to enable exact replication of comparisons. Overall the comparisons are useful but incomplete for a definitive claim of superiority over existing transfer methods. | B(4): The paper evaluates against a reasonable baseline (no-transfer monolingual) and an unrelated-language control (Arabic), uses the same data/metrics (SIGMORPHON/UniMorph, accuracy), and explicitly analyzes when gains reflect true linguistic transfer (e.g., cipher experiment) and acknowledges limitations. However, it appears to omit comparisons to other recent cross-lingual or paradigm-completion approaches that could be stronger baselines for transfer (beyond treating unrelated-language training as a control). Given thorough related-work discussion but some missing competitive baselines, the comparisons are good with minor omissions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper includes basic and informative baselines (monolingual no-transfer and an unrelated-language condition) and evaluates on multiple language pairs, which permits assessing the role of relatedness; it also analyzes confounds (e.g., ciphering) to separate true transfer from regularization. However, from the summary it appears to omit stronger or more recent cross-lingual and multilingual transfer baselines and alternative transfer techniques that would be natural competitors, and code/checkpoints are not mentioned to enable exact replication of comparisons. Overall the comparisons are useful but incomplete for a definitive claim of superiority over existing transfer methods.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates against a reasonable baseline (no-transfer monolingual) and an unrelated-language control (Arabic), uses the same data/metrics (SIGMORPHON/UniMorph, accuracy), and explicitly analyzes when gains reflect true linguistic transfer (e.g., cipher experiment) and acknowledges limitations. However, it appears to omit comparisons to other recent cross-lingual or paradigm-completion approaches that could be stronger baselines for transfer (beyond treating unrelated-language training as a control). Given thorough related-work discussion but some missing competitive baselines, the comparisons are good with minor omissions.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper sits squarely within the scope of a top NLP/ML conference: it tackles cross‑lingual transfer for morphological paradigm completion using neural sequence models and evaluates on established datasets (SIGMORPHON/UniMorph) across 21 language pairs. The methods (character‑level encoder‑decoder with attention, language tags, parameter tying) and reported evaluation (accuracy, hyperparameters, multi‑language experiments including zero/one‑shot regimes and related/unrelated baselines) match the standards and audience expectations for the venue. The work’s focus on low‑resource transfer, explicit analysis of language relatedness, and discussion of limitations are highly relevant to the community that values reproducible multilingual modeling. | B(5): This paper squarely targets core NLP/ML conference interests: cross-lingual transfer and morphologically informed generation, introducing a simple but effective one-shot transfer approach for paradigm completion with a character-level encoder-decoder and multilingual training. The experimental setup (21 language pairs across four families, SIGMORPHON/UniMorph schema, zero-/one-shot evaluations, and comparisons to no-transfer and unrelated-language baselines) and reported accuracy gains make it directly relevant to readers working on multilingual sequence models and low-resource morphology. Its explicit analysis of language relatedness and regularization effects speaks to the conference audience concerned with when transfer succeeds, and the framing and metrics align with community standards.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper sits squarely within the scope of a top NLP/ML conference: it tackles cross‑lingual transfer for morphological paradigm completion using neural sequence models and evaluates on established datasets (SIGMORPHON/UniMorph) across 21 language pairs. The methods (character‑level encoder‑decoder with attention, language tags, parameter tying) and reported evaluation (accuracy, hyperparameters, multi‑language experiments including zero/one‑shot regimes and related/unrelated baselines) match the standards and audience expectations for the venue. The work’s focus on low‑resource transfer, explicit analysis of language relatedness, and discussion of limitations are highly relevant to the community that values reproducible multilingual modeling.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper squarely targets core NLP/ML conference interests: cross-lingual transfer and morphologically informed generation, introducing a simple but effective one-shot transfer approach for paradigm completion with a character-level encoder-decoder and multilingual training. The experimental setup (21 language pairs across four families, SIGMORPHON/UniMorph schema, zero-/one-shot evaluations, and comparisons to no-transfer and unrelated-language baselines) and reported accuracy gains make it directly relevant to readers working on multilingual sequence models and low-resource morphology. Its explicit analysis of language relatedness and regularization effects speaks to the conference audience concerned with when transfer succeeds, and the framing and metrics align with community standards.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and largely supported by experiments across 21 language pairs showing up to 58% absolute accuracy gains and by ablations (e.g., unrelated-language and cipher experiments) that probe the source of transfer. Mathematical modeling is standard (encoder–decoder with attention) and appears complete for the task. However, the summary does not report multiple runs, variance estimates, or statistical tests, and code/checkpoints are not mentioned, which weakens reproducibility and leaves open whether some gains could be due to training variance or hyperparameter choices. Overall the methodology and controls (no-transfer and unrelated-language baselines) are sound, so only minor reproducibility and statistical-reporting gaps prevent a top score. | B(4): The paper’s main claims are generally well supported by empirical evidence: joint training with a single encoder–decoder across 21 language pairs yields substantial accuracy gains (up to 58%) and the authors include useful controls (no-transfer baseline, unrelated-language baseline, and a ciphered-source experiment) that probe whether gains reflect true linguistic transfer. Mathematical formulation is straightforward and there are no derivation issues in the summary; hyperparameters are reported. However, the summary does not mention statistical significance testing or more diverse baselines (e.g., alternative transfer architectures), and the admitted confounding role of unrelated-language data as a regularizer suggests some results could use deeper causal analysis. Overall the technical work is sound with only minor methodological gaps that do not overturn the main conclusions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and largely supported by experiments across 21 language pairs showing up to 58% absolute accuracy gains and by ablations (e.g., unrelated-language and cipher experiments) that probe the source of transfer. Mathematical modeling is standard (encoder–decoder with attention) and appears complete for the task. However, the summary does not report multiple runs, variance estimates, or statistical tests, and code/checkpoints are not mentioned, which weakens reproducibility and leaves open whether some gains could be due to training variance or hyperparameter choices. Overall the methodology and controls (no-transfer and unrelated-language baselines) are sound, so only minor reproducibility and statistical-reporting gaps prevent a top score.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s main claims are generally well supported by empirical evidence: joint training with a single encoder–decoder across 21 language pairs yields substantial accuracy gains (up to 58%) and the authors include useful controls (no-transfer baseline, unrelated-language baseline, and a ciphered-source experiment) that probe whether gains reflect true linguistic transfer. Mathematical formulation is straightforward and there are no derivation issues in the summary; hyperparameters are reported. However, the summary does not mention statistical significance testing or more diverse baselines (e.g., alternative transfer architectures), and the admitted confounding role of unrelated-language data as a regularizer suggests some results could use deeper causal analysis. Overall the technical work is sound with only minor methodological gaps that do not overturn the main conclusions.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper applies known neural sequence-to-sequence techniques (char-level encoder-decoder with attention, language tags, parameter tying / multi-task training) to the task of morphological paradigm completion and demonstrates one-shot/zero-shot cross-lingual transfer across 21 language pairs. Framed against prior multilingual transfer work, the methodological ingredients are not fundamentally new, but their targeted application to paradigm completion and the empirical demonstration (including sensitivity analyses like random-cipher and unrelated-language baselines) represent a useful, moderately novel contribution rather than a breakthrough. The authors clearly differentiate their contribution (one-shot/zero-shot paradigm completion experiments on SIGMORPHON/UniMorph data) from prior work, but the techniques are a non‑trivial yet incremental extension of existing multilingual sequence models. | B(4): The paper applies well-known neural sequence-to-sequence machinery (char-level encoder-decoder with attention and language tags) but uses it in a new, focused way for one-shot/zero-shot cross-lingual paradigm completion and provides empirical and diagnostic analyses across 21 language pairs that emphasize typological relatedness. While the modelling trick (shared parameters + language tag) is not novel per se, framing and demonstrating one-shot transfer for morphological paradigm completion — plus the ciphering and unrelated-language regularization analyses — meaningfully differentiates it from prior work and would inform experts about when cross-lingual morphological transfer succeeds.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper applies known neural sequence-to-sequence techniques (char-level encoder-decoder with attention, language tags, parameter tying / multi-task training) to the task of morphological paradigm completion and demonstrates one-shot/zero-shot cross-lingual transfer across 21 language pairs. Framed against prior multilingual transfer work, the methodological ingredients are not fundamentally new, but their targeted application to paradigm completion and the empirical demonstration (including sensitivity analyses like random-cipher and unrelated-language baselines) represent a useful, moderately novel contribution rather than a breakthrough. The authors clearly differentiate their contribution (one-shot/zero-shot paradigm completion experiments on SIGMORPHON/UniMorph data) from prior work, but the techniques are a non‑trivial yet incremental extension of existing multilingual sequence models.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper applies well-known neural sequence-to-sequence machinery (char-level encoder-decoder with attention and language tags) but uses it in a new, focused way for one-shot/zero-shot cross-lingual paradigm completion and provides empirical and diagnostic analyses across 21 language pairs that emphasize typological relatedness. While the modelling trick (shared parameters + language tag) is not novel per se, framing and demonstrating one-shot transfer for morphological paradigm completion — plus the ciphering and unrelated-language regularization analyses — meaningfully differentiates it from prior work and would inform experts about when cross-lingual morphological transfer succeeds.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well-organized with a clear flow from problem motivation through model formulation to experiments and conclusion, and the main contributions are stated early (novel cross-lingual transfer method, one/zero-shot results). Notation and method description (character-level encoder-decoder with language tags and tied parameters) are consistently presented, and figures/tables/examples appear informative; hyperparameters and related work are also reported thoroughly. Writing quality is good overall, though a few sections are dense and could be slightly clearer for readers less familiar with morphological paradigms. | B(4): The paper is overall well organized and clearly written: contributions are stated early, the method (character-level encoder–decoder with language tags) and experimental setup (21 language pairs, SIGMORPHON/UniMorph data) are presented in a logical sequence, and hyperparameters and related work are reported. Notation appears consistent and definitions adequate for a reader to follow the model and training procedure. Figures/tables and examples are mentioned as informative and the writing quality is good, though from the summary I infer there may be minor opportunities to better foreground linguistic intuitions (e.g., examples tying typological regularity to transfer outcomes) to further aid readers from morphology-focused backgrounds.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well-organized with a clear flow from problem motivation through model formulation to experiments and conclusion, and the main contributions are stated early (novel cross-lingual transfer method, one/zero-shot results). Notation and method description (character-level encoder-decoder with language tags and tied parameters) are consistently presented, and figures/tables/examples appear informative; hyperparameters and related work are also reported thoroughly. Writing quality is good overall, though a few sections are dense and could be slightly clearer for readers less familiar with morphological paradigms.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is overall well organized and clearly written: contributions are stated early, the method (character-level encoder–decoder with language tags) and experimental setup (21 language pairs, SIGMORPHON/UniMorph data) are presented in a logical sequence, and hyperparameters and related work are reported. Notation appears consistent and definitions adequate for a reader to follow the model and training procedure. Figures/tables and examples are mentioned as informative and the writing quality is good, though from the summary I infer there may be minor opportunities to better foreground linguistic intuitions (e.g., examples tying typological regularity to transfer outcomes) to further aid readers from morphology-focused backgrounds.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles an important practical problem—low-resource morphological paradigm completion—by demonstrating that a single multi-lingual encoder–decoder with language tags enables zero- and one-shot cross-lingual transfer, yielding up to 58% absolute accuracy gains across 21 language pairs. These empirical gains across multiple families substantiate a convincing case that parameter tying and joint training are an effective transfer mechanism, which will influence how researchers design low-resource morphological systems. The clear limitation that transferability depends strongly on language relatedness tempers broad generalization but also points to fertile follow-up work on quantifying and improving transfer across typologically distant languages. Given the demonstrated utility and concrete experimental evidence, this contribution is likely to be adopted and extended in the morphological and low-resource NLP communities. | B(4): This work addresses an important practical problem—low-resource morphological paradigm completion—by demonstrating a simple, trainable single-model cross-lingual transfer approach that yields large gains (up to 58% accuracy) across 21 language pairs. The finding that zero- and one-shot learning are achievable and that typological relatedness strongly governs transferability will change how researchers design transfer and multilingual training for morphology, and the clear analysis (including ciphering and unrelated-language regularization effects) opens concrete follow-up studies. While not entirely transformative, the method’s simplicity and empirical breadth make it likely to be widely adopted and to stimulate further research linking linguistic typology to transfer success.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important practical problem—low-resource morphological paradigm completion—by demonstrating that a single multi-lingual encoder–decoder with language tags enables zero- and one-shot cross-lingual transfer, yielding up to 58% absolute accuracy gains across 21 language pairs. These empirical gains across multiple families substantiate a convincing case that parameter tying and joint training are an effective transfer mechanism, which will influence how researchers design low-resource morphological systems. The clear limitation that transferability depends strongly on language relatedness tempers broad generalization but also points to fertile follow-up work on quantifying and improving transfer across typologically distant languages. Given the demonstrated utility and concrete experimental evidence, this contribution is likely to be adopted and extended in the morphological and low-resource NLP communities.

reviewer_b.justification: This work addresses an important practical problem—low-resource morphological paradigm completion—by demonstrating a simple, trainable single-model cross-lingual transfer approach that yields large gains (up to 58% accuracy) across 21 language pairs. The finding that zero- and one-shot learning are achievable and that typological relatedness strongly governs transferability will change how researchers design transfer and multilingual training for morphology, and the clear analysis (including ciphering and unrelated-language regularization effects) opens concrete follow-up studies. While not entirely transformative, the method’s simplicity and empirical breadth make it likely to be widely adopted and to stimulate further research linking linguistic typology to transfer success.

main_claims: Introduces a novel cross-lingual transfer method for paradigm completion using a single neural model to improve low-resource morphological generation.

Using labeled data from high-resource languages improves performance on low-resource languages, yielding up to 58% higher accuracy than no transfer in experiments on 21 language pairs from four families.
Shows that zero-shot and one-shot learning are possible for paradigm completion and that the degree of language relatedness strongly influences transferability.
_summary.methodology: Parameterize the conditional distribution p_theta with a character-level encoder-decoder gated RNN with attention, train jointly on high- and low-resource languages by prepending a language tag and tying parameters to enable transfer of morphological knowledge (multi-task learning).
limitations: Transfer of inflectional morphology is viable mainly among related languages; unrelated languages do not provide true transfer.
Unrelated-language training (e.g., Arabic) can act as a regularizer rather than providing true morphological transfer, confounding interpretation of gains.
Applying a random cipher to source-language data disrupts true linguistic transfer, showing sensitivity to surface-form correspondences.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - morphological paradigm completion / cross-lingual transfer

reviewer_a.name: Reproducible Methodologist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with extensive experience building and evaluating sequence-to-sequence models for NLP; has led reproducibility and benchmarking efforts in morphological generation and low-resource NLP.
traits: insists on clear, comparable baselines and thorough ablation studies that isolate each claimed contribution
looks for rigorous experimental design: dataset splits, hyperparameter search details, multiple runs and variance reporting, and statistical tests
values implementation transparency: exact model configurations, training regimes, preprocessing, and public code/checkpoints for reproducibility
checks for robustness across languages and data regimes (zero/one-shot) and sensitivity to choices like language tag encoding or parameter tying
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Evaluates papers primarily by the soundness and reproducibility of their experiments and the degree to which results support the claimed contributions.

reviewer_b.name: Morphology & Cross-lingual Impact Researcher
reviewer_b.background: Senior NLP researcher focused on morphological theory and cross-lingual transfer learning; has published on morphological paradigm learning and multilingual sequence models and advises work connecting computational methods to linguistic typology.
traits: assesses novelty and positioning relative to prior paradigm completion and multilingual transfer literature
values analyses that connect empirical findings to linguistic properties (e.g., typological relatedness, morphological complexity, regularity)
looks for clear articulation of practical impact: which low-resource languages benefit, limitations, and realistic deployment scenarios
appreciates both methodological simplicity that yields strong gains and principled explanations for when transfer will succeed or fail
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Judges work by how it advances understanding and practice in morphological and cross-lingual NLP, emphasizing novelty, linguistic insight, and real-world relevance.
